It always slows down queries
It creates duplicate data
Partitioning divides data into segments based on key columns, allowing queries to scan only relevant partitions instead of the entire dataset

intermediate.question: What is the difference between structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data in big data?
options: They are all the same format
Structured has fixed schema (tables), semi-structured has flexible schema (JSON/XML), and unstructured has no schema (images/text)
Only structured data is useful
Unstructured data cannot be processed

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of Apache Airflow in big data workflows?
options: It replaces Apache Spark
It processes data in real-time
It provides air conditioning for data centers
Airflow is a workflow orchestration platform that schedules, monitors, and manages complex data pipeline dependencies as directed acyclic graphs

intermediate.question: What is the concept of data locality in distributed computing?
options: Data must always be moved to a central server
Data locality only matters for small datasets
Storing all data in one location
Moving computation to where the data resides rather than moving large datasets to the compute nodes, minimizing network transfer

intermediate.question: What is the difference between concept drift and data drift?
options: They are identical phenomena
Data drift does not require monitoring
Data drift is a change in input feature distributions while concept drift is a change in the relationship between features and the target variable
Concept drift only affects deep learning

intermediate.question: How does the shadow deployment pattern work for ML models?
options: It replaces the model immediately
Shadow models never see real data
Models run in the dark
The new model receives production traffic and generates predictions alongside the existing model, but only the old model's predictions are served while the new model's performance is monitored

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a model registry in MLOps?
options: A list of model architectures
A centralized repository that stores, versions, tracks, and manages ML model artifacts, metadata, and lifecycle stages
A training data storage system
A government registration system

intermediate.question: What is infrastructure as code and why is it important for MLOps?
options: IaC replaces all manual processes
Defining and managing infrastructure through code files, enabling reproducible, version-controlled, and automated provisioning of ML environments
It only applies to web development
Writing code on physical infrastructure

intermediate.question: How does model compression benefit edge deployment?
options: Compression always reduces accuracy unacceptably
It makes models larger
Edge devices have unlimited resources
Techniques like pruning, quantization, and distillation reduce model size and computation, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices like mobile phones and IoT sensors

intermediate.question: What is the role of feature flags in ML model deployment?
options: They only work for software features
Flags used in feature engineering
Feature flags enable gradually enabling new models for specific user segments, allowing controlled rollout and quick disabling if issues arise
Feature flags are identical to A/B tests

intermediate.question: What is the difference between online and offline model evaluation?
options: They produce identical results
Online evaluation is always more accurate
Offline evaluation uses production data
Offline evaluation uses held-out historical data while online evaluation measures performance on live production traffic with real user interactions

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of model explainability tools in production?
options: Explainability is only for research
To provide human-understandable explanations for individual predictions, supporting debugging, regulatory compliance, and user trust
All models are inherently explainable
To make models more complex

intermediate.question: What is the concept of model staleness in production?
options: A model becoming less accurate over time as the real-world data distribution changes from the data it was trained on
Staleness only affects neural networks
Models always improve with age
Models that smell bad

intermediate.question: What is the concept of algorithmic recourse and why is it important?
options: Recourse is only relevant for recommendation systems
Recourse means repeating algorithms
Algorithmic recourse provides individuals with actionable steps to change an unfavorable AI decision, ensuring people are not permanently harmed by automated systems
It is a type of optimization algorithm

intermediate.question: How can feedback loops amplify bias in AI systems?
options: Feedback loops always reduce bias
Biased AI decisions influence future data collection which reinforces the original bias, creating a cycle where the system becomes increasingly biased over time
Feedback loops do not exist in AI
They only affect recommendation systems

intermediate.question: What is the difference between explainability and interpretability in AI?
options: Interpretability means a model is inherently understandable while explainability provides post-hoc explanations for complex models that are not inherently transparent
They are identical concepts
Interpretability only applies to neural networks
Explainability is always preferred

intermediate.question: What is the concept of privacy-preserving machine learning?
options: Techniques that enable training and inference on sensitive data while protecting individual privacy, including federated learning, differential privacy, and secure computation
Privacy cannot be preserved in ML
Making ML models private property
It only applies to healthcare data

intermediate.question: What are model cards and why are they important?
options: They are used for trading models
Standardized documentation describing a model's intended use, performance metrics, limitations, and ethical considerations, promoting transparency and responsible deployment
Model cards replace all other documentation
Physical cards with model images

intermediate.question: What is the problem of proxy discrimination in AI systems?
options: It only happens with simple models
Proxy discrimination cannot occur in AI
Using proxy servers for AI
When AI uses seemingly neutral features that are highly correlated with protected attributes like race or gender, resulting in discriminatory outcomes despite not explicitly using those attributes

intermediate.question: How does the concept of AI safety differ from AI security?
options: They are identical concerns
AI safety focuses on preventing unintended harmful behaviors from AI systems themselves, while AI security protects AI systems from external malicious attacks
Safety is only about physical robots
Security is not relevant to AI

intermediate.question: What is the right to erasure and how does it apply to AI systems?
options: Erasing AI code permanently
It does not apply to AI
Under GDPR, individuals can request deletion of their personal data, which creates challenges for AI systems trained on that data as the model may retain learned patterns
Only applies to social media

intermediate.question: What is the environmental impact of training large AI models?
options: Training large models consumes significant energy and produces carbon emissions, with some large model training runs emitting as much CO2 as multiple transatlantic flights
Only GPU manufacturing has environmental impact
Environmental impact is negligible
AI training has zero environmental impact

intermediate.question: What is the concept of AI value alignment?
options: Value alignment is not important for AI
Ensuring AI systems' goals, behaviors, and decisions are consistent with human values, intentions, and ethical principles
Aligning data values in a database
Aligning model parameters to specific values

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a staging environment in ML deployment?
options: A pre-production environment that mirrors production for testing models with realistic data and infrastructure before live deployment
A training environment for data scientists
A storage area for old models
A theater stage for AI presentations

intermediate.question: What is the primary goal of machine learning?
options: To program explicit rules for every task
To store large amounts of data
To replace all human workers
To enable computers to learn patterns from data and make predictions without being explicitly programmed
intermediate.explanation: Machine learning focuses on building algorithms that learn from and make predictions on data, improving their performance through experience rather than following hard-coded rules.

intermediate.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of machine learning?
options: Unsupervised learning
Supervised learning
Reinforcement learning
Compiled learning
intermediate.explanation: The three main types of ML are supervised (labeled data), unsupervised (unlabeled data), and reinforcement learning (reward-based). 'Compiled learning' does not exist.

intermediate.question: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is best defined as:
options: A specific programming language
The simulation of human intelligence processes by computer systems, including learning, reasoning, and self-correction
Only robotics
A type of database
intermediate.explanation: AI is a broad field encompassing systems that can perform tasks requiring human-like intelligence, including perception, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, and natural language understanding.

intermediate.question: In linear algebra, a matrix multiplication of an m x n matrix with an n x p matrix produces:
options: A p x m matrix
An m x n matrix
An m x p matrix
An n x n matrix
intermediate.explanation: When multiplying matrices A (m x n) and B (n x p), the inner dimensions must match (n), and the result is an m x p matrix.

intermediate.question: Bayes' theorem is used in machine learning to:
options: Measure distance between points
Normalize data
Update the probability of a hypothesis based on new evidence
Calculate matrix inverses
intermediate.explanation: Bayes' theorem calculates posterior probability: P(H|E) = P(E|H) * P(H) / P(E). It forms the basis of Naive Bayes classifiers and Bayesian inference in ML.

intermediate.question: Which Python library is most commonly used for numerical array operations in data science?
options: Flask
Django
NumPy
Requests
intermediate.explanation: NumPy provides support for large, multi-dimensional arrays and matrices, along with mathematical functions to operate on them efficiently. It is the backbone of most data science libraries.

intermediate.question: Pandas in Python is primarily used for:
options: Data manipulation and analysis using DataFrames and Series
Web development
Network programming
Image processing
intermediate.explanation: Pandas provides DataFrames (2D labeled data) and Series (1D labeled data) for cleaning, transforming, merging, and analyzing structured data.

intermediate.question: Which Python library is used for creating statistical visualizations?
options: Scikit-learn
Matplotlib / Seaborn
TensorFlow
NumPy
intermediate.explanation: Matplotlib provides basic plotting functions, while Seaborn builds on it for statistical visualizations like heatmaps, distribution plots, and pair plots.

intermediate.question: Handling missing values in a dataset can be done by:
options: Doubling the existing values
Ignoring them completely
Only deleting the entire column
Deleting rows with missing values, imputing with mean/median/mode, or using prediction models
intermediate.explanation: Common strategies include: deletion (listwise/pairwise), imputation (mean, median, mode, KNN imputation), or using algorithms that handle missing values natively.

intermediate.question: Feature scaling is important because:
options: It makes the code run faster
Some ML algorithms (e.g., KNN, SVM, gradient descent) are sensitive to the magnitude of features and perform poorly without scaling
It removes outliers
It adds more data to the dataset
intermediate.explanation: Feature scaling (normalization or standardization) ensures all features contribute equally to distance calculations and gradient updates. Without it, features with larger ranges dominate.

intermediate.question: One-hot encoding is used to:
options: Reduce the number of features
Encrypt data
Sort data alphabetically
Convert categorical variables into binary vectors so ML algorithms can process them
intermediate.explanation: One-hot encoding creates a binary column for each category. For example, a 'Color' column with values [Red, Blue, Green] becomes three binary columns: is_Red, is_Blue, is_Green.

intermediate.question: What is the typical train-test split ratio used in machine learning?
intermediate.explanation: Common splits are 80/20 or 70/30 (training/testing). A validation set (e.g., 60/20/20) may be added for hyperparameter tuning. K-fold cross-validation is another approach.

intermediate.question: Label encoding assigns:
options: Floating point values to each category
Binary vectors to each category
A unique integer to each category of a categorical variable
Random numbers to categories
intermediate.explanation: Label encoding maps each unique category to an integer (e.g., Red=0, Blue=1, Green=2). It is suitable for ordinal features but can mislead models for nominal features since it implies order.

intermediate.question: A correlation heatmap is used in EDA to:
options: Visualize the strength and direction of linear relationships between pairs of numerical features
Train a model
Compress data files
Deploy an application
intermediate.explanation: A correlation heatmap shows pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients as colored cells. Values near +1 indicate strong positive correlation, near -1 strong negative, and near 0 weak/no linear relationship.

intermediate.question: A box plot displays:
options: The distribution of data showing median, quartiles, and potential outliers
Only the mean
Accuracy of a model
The correlation between two variables
intermediate.explanation: A box plot shows the five-number summary: minimum, Q1 (25th percentile), median (

intermediate.question: What is the primary purpose of EDA?
options: To deploy the final model
To write production code
To understand the structure, patterns, anomalies, and relationships in data before modeling
To encrypt the dataset
intermediate.explanation: EDA uses statistics and visualizations (histograms, scatter plots, heatmaps) to understand data distributions, detect outliers, spot missing values, and identify feature relationships before building models.

intermediate.question: A histogram is used in EDA to:
options: Calculate model accuracy
Perform feature selection
Display the frequency distribution of a single numerical variable
Show relationships between two variables
intermediate.explanation: A histogram divides the range of a continuous variable into bins and shows the count (or density) of observations in each bin, revealing the shape of the distribution (normal, skewed, bimodal, etc.).

intermediate.question: In supervised learning, the training data consists of:
options: Unlabeled images only
Input features paired with corresponding output labels (target variable)
Only input features without any labels
Random noise
intermediate.explanation: Supervised learning uses labeled data where each training example has input features (X) and a known output label (y). The model learns the mapping from X to y.

intermediate.question: Linear regression predicts:
options: A continuous numerical output based on input features
Image categories
Cluster assignments
Categorical class labels
intermediate.explanation: Linear regression models the relationship between features and a continuous target variable by fitting a line (or hyperplane) that minimizes the sum of squared errors.

intermediate.question: Logistic regression is used for:
options: Dimensionality reduction
Predicting continuous values
Clustering
Binary or multi-class classification problems, outputting probabilities
intermediate.explanation: Despite its name, logistic regression is a classification algorithm. It uses the sigmoid function to output probabilities between 0 and 1, then applies a threshold for classification.

intermediate.question: A Decision Tree makes predictions by:
options: Recursively splitting the data based on feature values that best separate the target classes
Using neural network layers
Random guessing
Calculating distances between points
intermediate.explanation: Decision Trees split data at each node using the feature and threshold that maximize information gain (or minimize impurity/Gini index), creating a tree of decisions leading to predictions.

intermediate.question: Support Vector Machine (SVM) finds:
options: The number of features to remove
The mean of all features
The optimal hyperplane that maximizes the margin between two classes
The cluster centroids
intermediate.explanation: SVM finds the decision boundary (hyperplane) that maximizes the distance (margin) between the closest data points of different classes (support vectors). Kernel tricks handle non-linear boundaries.

intermediate.question: Which metric is best for evaluating a regression model?
options: F1-score
Mean Squared Error (MSE) or R-squared
Accuracy
Confusion matrix
intermediate.explanation: Regression metrics measure the difference between predicted and actual continuous values. MSE averages squared errors; R-squared measures how well the model explains variance. Accuracy is for classification.

intermediate.question: Random Forest is an ensemble method that:
options: Only works with text data
Combines multiple decision trees trained on random subsets of data and features, then aggregates their predictions
Is a type of neural network
Uses a single decision tree
intermediate.explanation: Random Forest uses bagging: it trains many decision trees on bootstrapped samples with random feature subsets, then averages predictions (regression) or takes majority vote (classification), reducing overfitting.

intermediate.question: Gradient Boosting builds models by:
options: Using only one model
Training all trees independently in parallel
Removing features one by one
Sequentially adding models that correct the errors of previous models, minimizing a loss function
intermediate.explanation: Gradient Boosting trains weak learners (typically shallow trees) sequentially. Each new tree focuses on the residual errors from the previous ensemble, gradually improving predictions.

intermediate.question: XGBoost is a popular ML library because it:
options: Only works for text data
Provides an optimized, scalable implementation of gradient boosting with regularization to prevent overfitting
Replaces neural networks entirely
Requires no hyperparameter tuning
intermediate.explanation: XGBoost adds L1/L2 regularization to gradient boosting, handles missing values, supports parallel processing, and includes built-in cross-validation, making it a top choice for tabular data competitions.

intermediate.question: K-Means clustering algorithm:
options: Is a supervised algorithm
Partitions data into K clusters by minimizing the sum of squared distances between points and their cluster centroids
Requires labeled data
Predicts continuous values
intermediate.explanation: K-Means iteratively assigns each data point to the nearest centroid and recalculates centroids until convergence. It requires specifying K (number of clusters) in advance.

intermediate.question: Principal Component Analysis (PC
options: Reducing the dimensionality of data while preserving maximum variance
Web development
Generating synthetic data
is used for: A) Classification
intermediate.explanation: PCA transforms high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional space by finding principal components (orthogonal directions of maximum variance), reducing features while retaining the most information.

intermediate.question: The elbow method in K-Means is used to:
options: Scale features
Remove outliers
Train the model faster
Determine the optimal number of clusters (K) by plotting within-cluster sum of squares against K values
intermediate.explanation: The elbow method plots WCSS (within-cluster sum of squares) for different K values. The 'elbow' point where the rate of decrease sharply changes suggests the optimal K.

intermediate.question: The F1-score is the harmonic mean of:
options: Bias and variance
Precision and recall
Accuracy and specificity
Training and test error
intermediate.explanation: F1 = 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall). It balances precision (of positive predictions, how many are correct) and recall (of actual positives, how many were found), useful for imbalanced classes.

intermediate.question: Overfitting occurs when a model:
options: Performs poorly on both training and test data
Has too few parameters
Learns the training data too well (including noise) and performs poorly on unseen test data
Is too simple to capture patterns
intermediate.explanation: An overfit model memorizes training data, achieving high training accuracy but low test accuracy. It fails to generalize. Solutions include regularization, more data, simpler models, or dropout.

intermediate.question: K-fold cross-validation works by:
options: Removing K features
Splitting data into K folds, training on K-1 folds and validating on the remaining fold, rotating K times
Using only the test set
Training on 100% of the data
intermediate.explanation: K-fold CV ensures every data point is used for both training and validation. The average performance across K folds gives a more robust estimate of model generalization than a single train-test split.

intermediate.question: The confusion matrix for a binary classifier shows:
options: Feature importance scores
Training time
True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives
Only accuracy
intermediate.explanation: A confusion matrix is a 2x2 table showing TP (correctly predicted positive), TN (correctly predicted negative), FP (incorrectly predicted positive), and FN (incorrectly predicted negative).

intermediate.question: The ROC-AUC score measures:
options: Training speed
The model's ability to distinguish between classes across all classification thresholds
Memory consumption
The number of features used
intermediate.explanation: The ROC curve plots True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at various thresholds. AUC (Area Under Curve) summarizes performance: 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is random guessing.

intermediate.question: Feature engineering is the process of:
options: Deleting all features
Only selecting features from a predefined list
Running the model without features
Creating new informative features from existing data to improve model performance
intermediate.explanation: Feature engineering transforms raw data into meaningful features, such as extracting day-of-week from a date, creating interaction terms, binning continuous variables, or computing aggregates.

intermediate.question: Which technique selects features by removing those with low variance?
options: Variance Threshold
Gradient Boosting
PCA
K-Means
intermediate.explanation: Variance Threshold is a simple filter method that removes features whose variance falls below a specified threshold. Features with near-zero variance carry little predictive information.

intermediate.question: Regularization (L1/L2) in machine learning is used to:
options: Speed up training only
Add more training data
Increase the number of features
Penalize large model coefficients to prevent overfitting and improve generalization
intermediate.explanation: L1 (Lasso) adds absolute value penalty pushing some weights to zero (feature selection). L2 (Ridge) adds squared penalty shrinking weights. Both discourage complex models that overfit.

intermediate.question: An artificial neural network consists of:
options: Only decision trees
Layers of interconnected neurons (input, hidden, and output layers) that learn through backpropagation
A single regression equation
Only one neuron
intermediate.explanation: Neural networks have an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Each neuron applies weights, bias, and an activation function. Backpropagation adjusts weights to minimize loss.

intermediate.question: The activation function ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) outputs:
options: Values between -1 and 1 only
Only 0 or 1
max(0, x), returning zero for negative inputs and x for positive inputs
Always negative values
intermediate.explanation: ReLU(x) = max(0, x). It is the most widely used activation function in deep learning because it reduces the vanishing gradient problem and is computationally efficient.

intermediate.question: Backpropagation in neural networks is used to:
options: Forward-pass the data only
Calculate gradients of the loss function with respect to each weight, enabling weight updates via gradient descent
Initialize weights randomly
Preprocess data
intermediate.explanation: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss function layer by layer (using the chain rule), then gradient descent updates each weight to minimize the loss.

intermediate.question: A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is primarily used for:
options: Image recognition and computer vision tasks, using convolutional filters to detect spatial features
Natural language processing only
Audio compression
Tabular data only
intermediate.explanation: CNNs apply learnable convolutional filters that detect spatial patterns (edges, textures, objects) in images. Pooling layers reduce spatial dimensions, and fully connected layers make final predictions.

intermediate.question: Dropout is a regularization technique in neural networks that:
options: Drops the learning rate
Removes input features permanently
Randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during training to prevent overfitting
Removes entire layers
intermediate.explanation: Dropout randomly sets a fraction of neuron outputs to zero during each training step, forcing the network to learn redundant representations. This reduces co-adaptation and prevents overfitting.

intermediate.question: The vanishing gradient problem in deep networks means:
options: Gradients become too large
Data is lost during forward pass
The model trains too quickly
Gradients become extremely small in early layers during backpropagation, making those layers learn very slowly
intermediate.explanation: With certain activation functions (like sigmoid), gradients shrink exponentially through layers during backpropagation, so early layers barely update. ReLU and skip connections (ResNets) help mitigate this.

intermediate.question: A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is designed for:
options: Sequential data (time series, text, speech) where the output depends on previous computations
Database queries
File compression
Static image classification only
intermediate.explanation: RNNs maintain hidden states that capture information from previous time steps, making them suitable for sequences. However, they suffer from vanishing gradients, which LSTMs and GRUs address.

intermediate.question: Transfer learning involves:
options: Training a model from scratch for every new task
Reusing a pre-trained model (trained on a large dataset) and fine-tuning it for a new, related task
Transferring data between servers
Deleting model weights
intermediate.explanation: Transfer learning leverages knowledge from a model trained on a large dataset (e.g., ImageNet). The pre-trained layers extract general features, and only the final layers are retrained for the new task.

intermediate.question: Tokenization in NLP is:
options: Breaking text into smaller units (words, subwords, or characters) for processing
Encrypting text data
Translating text to another language
Removing all text from a document
intermediate.explanation: Tokenization splits raw text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters). It is the first step in most NLP pipelines, converting text into a format that ML models can process.

intermediate.question: TF-IDF stands for:
options: Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency
Text Format-Indexed Data File
Total Feature-Input Data Function
Text Frequency-Inverse Data Format
intermediate.explanation: TF-IDF measures how important a word is to a document relative to a corpus. TF counts word frequency in a document; IDF downweights words that appear in many documents (common words like 'the').

intermediate.question: Word embeddings (e.g., Word2Vec, GloVe) convert words into:
options: Dense numerical vectors that capture semantic relationships between words
HTML tags
Binary codes
Audio signals
intermediate.explanation: Word embeddings map words to dense vectors in a continuous space where semantically similar words are closer together. For example, 'king' - 'man' + 'woman' is approximately 'queen'.

intermediate.question: Sentiment analysis is an NLP task that:
options: Determines the emotional tone (positive, negative, neutral) expressed in a piece of text
Translates text between languages
Counts the words in a document
Encrypts messages
intermediate.explanation: Sentiment analysis classifies text by opinion polarity. It is widely used for analyzing product reviews, social media posts, and customer feedback to gauge public opinion.

intermediate.question: In image classification, a CNN learns to:
options: Only resize images
Compress images only
Convert images to audio
Automatically extract hierarchical features from images and assign them to predefined categories
intermediate.explanation: CNNs learn hierarchical features: early layers detect edges and textures, middle layers detect parts and patterns, and deeper layers recognize complete objects, ultimately classifying the image.

intermediate.question: Data augmentation in computer vision involves:
options: Deleting training images
Reducing image resolution
Applying transformations (rotation, flipping, scaling, cropping) to training images to increase dataset diversity
Only collecting more images
intermediate.explanation: Data augmentation artificially increases training data by applying random transformations. This helps prevent overfitting and improves generalization, especially when labeled data is limited.

intermediate.question: The '3 Vs' of Big Data refer to:
options: Version, Validation, Verification
Vectorization, Visualization, Virtualization
Volume, Velocity, Variety
Vision, Value, Versatility
intermediate.explanation: Big Data is characterized by Volume (massive amount of data), Velocity (speed of data generation and processing), and Variety (different types: structured, semi-structured, unstructured).

intermediate.question: Apache Hadoop is primarily used for:
options: Email management
Image editing
Web development
Distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of computers
intermediate.explanation: Hadoop provides HDFS (distributed file storage) and MapReduce (distributed processing) for handling massive datasets across commodity hardware clusters.

intermediate.question: Apache Spark differs from Hadoop MapReduce mainly because it:
options: Does not support Python
Only works on single machines
Cannot handle large datasets
Processes data in-memory, making it much faster for iterative ML algorithms
intermediate.explanation: Spark performs in-memory computation, avoiding the disk I/O overhead of MapReduce. This makes it up to 100x faster for iterative tasks like machine learning, while still supporting distributed processing.

intermediate.question: MLOps is:
options: A database system
A type of neural network
A programming language
A set of practices combining ML, DevOps, and data engineering to deploy and maintain ML models in production reliably
intermediate.explanation: MLOps covers the full ML lifecycle: data versioning, experiment tracking, model training, testing, deployment, monitoring, and retraining, ensuring models perform reliably in production.

intermediate.question: Model serving refers to:
options: Training a model
Deleting a model
Making a trained model available to receive input data and return predictions, typically via an API
Visualizing model weights
intermediate.explanation: Model serving deploys a trained model as a service (often a REST API) so applications can send input data and receive predictions in real time or in batch mode.

intermediate.question: Bias in AI models can occur when:
options: The learning rate is too high
Training data does not represent the real-world population fairly, or reflects historical prejudices
The model uses too many layers
The model is deployed on the cloud
intermediate.explanation: If training data underrepresents certain groups or encodes historical biases, the model will learn and amplify those biases, leading to unfair predictions for underrepresented populations.

intermediate.question: Explainability in AI refers to:
options: Making AI models run faster
Encrypting model weights
Reducing model size
The ability to understand and interpret how and why a model makes specific predictions or decisions
intermediate.explanation: Explainable AI (XAI) uses techniques like SHAP, LIME, and feature importance to make model decisions transparent and interpretable, which is critical for trust, accountability, and regulatory compliance.

expert.question: The Chinese Room argument by John Searle challenges:
options: Modern machine learning training algorithms
Standard statistical data analytics methods
Strong AI - that machines can truly understand
Weak AI - that machines can mimic intelligence

expert.question: Which of the following problems is considered AI-complete?
options: Performing a binary search
Computing matrix multiplication
Natural language understanding
Sorting a list of numbers

expert.question: The frame problem in AI refers to:
options: Difficulty in determining what changes and what stays the same after an action
Complications of managing memory allocation in modern computers
Problems with optimizing database indexing for query performance
Challenges of reducing network latency across distributed systems

expert.question: Which approach to AI attempts to mimic biological neural structures?
options: Connectionism
Bayesian methods
Symbolism
Evolutionary computation

expert.question: In the context of AI, what is the combinatorial explosion?
options: A specialized type of deep neural network architecture
Rapid growth of possible solutions making brute force infeasible
An advanced technique for compressing large data files
A sudden failure in hardware caused by electrical overload

expert.question: What distinguishes Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) from Narrow AI?
options: AGI is faster at one specific computational task
AGI consumes significantly less working memory
AGI requires absolutely no input training data
AGI can perform any intellectual task a human can

expert.question: The symbol grounding problem in AI concerns:
options: How symbols in an AI system get their meaning
How to increase the clock speed of a processor
How to efficiently compress large data archives
How to optimize the speed of sorting algorithms

expert.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a multi-agent system?
options: A single centralized processing unit
No communication between components
Only one autonomous decision-maker
Multiple interacting intelligent agents

expert.question: What is the knowledge representation bottleneck in AI?
options: Difficulty in encoding real-world knowledge into a formal system
Insufficient physical storage space on server hardware
Limited pixel resolution on visual display monitors
Slow data transfer speeds across network connections

expert.question: In the context of data analytics, what is the CRISP-DM model?
options: A multi-layer neural network architecture design
A general-purpose interpreted programming language
A scalable relational database management system
A standard process model for data mining projects

expert.question: The Hessian matrix contains:
options: Second-order partial derivatives
First-order derivatives only
Eigenvalues of the matrix only
The determinant values only

expert.question: The Kullback-Leibler divergence measures:
options: The central tendency or mean of a distribution
The spread or variance of a single distribution
The linear correlation between two variables
How one probability distribution differs from another

expert.question: A Jacobian matrix represents:
options: Only the output values of a single scalar function
Only the eigenvalues of a square transformation matrix
First-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function
Only the second-order derivatives of a scalar function

expert.question: In the context of ML, why is the log-likelihood used instead of likelihood?
options: It produces more statistically accurate final estimates
It converts products to sums, making computation easier
It runs faster on modern parallel computing hardware
It requires significantly less memory during training
